seoTitle: How to Embed the Zendesk Help Center in Your Website
metaDescription: Learn how to embed the Zendesk Help Center in a website using the messaging Web Widget, so visitors can search articles without leaving your page.
intro: Sending visitors away from your site just to read an FAQ hurts conversions and loses traffic. Zendesk's messaging Web Widget can surface Help Center search and articles directly inside a pop-up container on your own pages. This guide covers enabling the widget's knowledge search and installing the script.

steps.heading: Open messaging channel settings
steps.body: In Admin Center, expand the Channels menu and select Messaging Options.

steps.heading: Enable Help Center search
steps.body: Open your active web channel, go to the Responses tab, and turn on Help Center capabilities so it feeds articles into the widget.

steps.heading: Style the widget bubble
steps.body: Move to the Style tab to personalize widget colors, layout placement, and launcher icons.

steps.heading: Copy the script tag
steps.body: Open the Installation section and click the copy icon to grab your unique script tag block.

steps.heading: Paste it into your site
steps.body: Open your website's template source files and paste the snippet right before the closing body tag.

steps.heading: Confirm it works
steps.body: Save your site changes, launch your live URL, and confirm the help bubble opens correctly.

faq.question: Does this replace a standalone Help Center page?
faq.answer: No, it surfaces the same articles inside a widget on your existing pages, in addition to your standalone Help Center.

faq.question: Can visitors submit a ticket from the same widget?
faq.answer: Yes, the messaging widget can offer quick ticket forms alongside search and article suggestions.

faq.question: Will the widget slow down my site?
faq.answer: The script is designed to load asynchronously, but placing it right before the closing body tag helps avoid render delays.

faq.question: Can I use this on a WordPress or custom CMS site?
faq.answer: Yes, the same script snippet works across WordPress, custom HTML, or most CMS platforms.
